Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Lai Siu Chiu J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: Jumabhoy Rafiq

Facts.Text: Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd was incorporated in May 1991 to hold the Jumabhoy family’s stake in Scotts Holding Ltd, a public listed company.
Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd held the Jumabhoy family’s stake in Scotts Holding Ltd, a public listed company.
One of the main subsidiaries of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd was Lion City Holdings Pte Ltd.
Lion City Holdings Pte Ltd was a subsidiary of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
Rafiq Jumabhoy is the son of Ameerali R Jumabhoy and the brother of Iqbal Jumabhoy and Assad Jumabhoy.
Yusuf Jumabhoy and Mustafa Jumabhoy are brothers of Ameerali R Jumabhoy and uncles of Rafiq Jumabhoy.
Rafiq Jumabhoy was a director of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On 27 July 1996, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd resolved that Yusuf Jumabhoy and Rafiq Jumabhoy be appointed from among the directors to review the company’s investment in Lion City Holdings Pte Ltd, determine steps to safeguard that investment and related amounts, and exercise all powers of the board in connection with that review.

On 27 July 1996, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd authorised Yusuf Jumabhoy and Rafiq Jumabhoy to decide questions, take steps, approve matters, obtain reports or data, instruct Rajah & Tann as solicitors and other professional advisers, and exercise discretion necessary to give effect to their review of Lion City Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 27 July 1996, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd resolved that the company shall indemnify Yusuf Jumabhoy and Rafiq Jumabhoy for all costs and expenses incurred by them personally in respect of their appointment.
On 27 July 1996, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd appointed Rajah & Tann as the company’s solicitors to act in relation to all matters regarding Lion City Holdings Pte Ltd.

On 6 August 1996, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd passed a resolution, in all material respects similar to the 27 July 1996 resolution, relating to the company’s investment in SHL.
On 14 May 1996, while in South America, Rafiq Jumabhoy wrote to Yusuf Jumabhoy and raised the question of his remuneration, offering his services to SIS and asking for a fixed monthly pay of $25,000.
On 14 May 1996, Rafiq Jumabhoy sent a letter to Yusuf Jumabhoy raising the question of his remuneration and proposing a fixed monthly pay of $25,000 for his services to SIS.

On 18 May 1996, in a letter from Rafiq Jumabhoy to Yusuf Jumabhoy, Rafiq referred to a conversation with Andrew Smith, an investment consultant of SIS, in which he said he was still awaiting a reply to his letter of 14 May 1996.
On 18 May 1996, Rafiq Jumabhoy referred to a conversation with Andrew Smith, an investment consultant of SIS, and said he was still awaiting a reply to his 14 May 1996 letter.
By the mid-1990s, members of the Jumabhoy family, including Rajabali Jumabhoy, were involved in protracted legal proceedings arising from a financial crisis in Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
By the mid-1990s, banks that had granted facilities to Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd became alarmed due to a financial crisis in the company.
By the mid-1990s, members of the Jumabhoy family were concerned that banks might take action against Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
During the period of financial difficulties, Yusuf Jumabhoy and Rafiq Jumabhoy were expected to deal with the banks in relation to the company’s affairs.

On 18 June 1997, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd resolved that, in consideration for his taking action for the recovery of the assets of the company, Rafiq Jumabhoy be fully indemnified for all costs and expenses incurred and against all legal liability as far as permitted by law with regards to LCH, its subsidiaries and associates, and in the pursuit of any action to be taken on behalf of SIS/LCH as advised by the company’s solicitors.

On 30 June 1997, an indemnity document was issued stating that, in consideration of Rafiq Jumabhoy agreeing to reasonably assist at the cost and expense of the relevant company and/or ourselves in the recovery of the assets of SIS, LCH and/or its subsidiaries and associated companies, the prosecution of any claim which any company of the LCH Group may have, and any reasonable action in connection with the affairs of any company of the LCH Group as may be advised by the company’s solicitors, the issuers agreed to indemnify him out of their assets against all costs and expenses and liabilities incurred or suffered by him in connection with those matters until revocation in writing.

On 30 June 1997, Rafiq Jumabhoy received an indemnity in which the issuers agreed to keep him indemnified, to the extent permitted by law, out of their assets against all costs, expenses and liabilities incurred or suffered by him in connection with assisting with recovery of assets, prosecution of claims, and actions connected with the affairs of companies in the LCH Group.

On 9 October 1997, an indemnity document was issued stating that, in consideration of Rafiq Jumabhoy agreeing to undertake duties as nominee director of Rosebury Holdings Limited, a New Zealand incorporated company, the issuers would indemnify him against all claims, demands, payments, actions, suits, proceedings, costs, expenses and other liabilities arising out of such service.

On 9 October 1997, Rafiq Jumabhoy was granted an indemnity in connection with his agreement to undertake duties as nominee director of Rosebury Holdings Limited, covering claims, demands, payments, proceedings, costs, expenses and other liabilities arising out of that service.

The Articles of Association of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd included Article 77, which provided that directors other than a managing director shall be paid remuneration for their services out of company funds at such rate as the company in general meeting may determine, with such remuneration divided among them as the directors determine or, in default, equally.

The Articles of Association of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd included Article 80, which provided that each director shall be paid travelling, hotel and other expenses properly incurred, and that if any director is called upon to perform extra services for company purposes, the company shall remunerate that director by a fixed sum determined by the directors, which may be in addition to or in substitution for his share in the general remuneration.
By 2002, Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd was under compulsory liquidation.
In 2002, Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd instituted Suit No 736 of 2002 against, among others, Rafiq Jumabhoy, Ameerali R Jumabhoy and Iqbal Jumabhoy.

Rafiq Jumabhoy’s counterclaim for work done and services rendered and expenses incurred on account of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd was stated to be pursuant to the board resolutions dated 27 July 1996, 6 August 1996 and 18 June 1997, and the written indemnities dated 30 June 1997 and 9 October 1997.

Rafiq Jumabhoy’s counterclaim for work done and services rendered and expenses incurred on account of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd was stated to be pursuant to the board resolutions dated 27 July 1996, 6 August 1996 and 18 June 1997, and the written indemnities dated 30 June 1997 and 9 October 1997.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether, based on the board resolutions of 27 July 1996, 6 August 1996 and 18 June 1997 and the indemnities dated 30 June 1997 and 9 October 1997, the term "indemnify for all costs and expenses" included payment to a director for time spent on company affairs in addition to reimbursement of out-of-pocket expenses, and whether the director could obtain remuneration on a quantum meruit basis despite specific remuneration provisions in the company’s articles of association.
Party_Details.Rule: In company law, a director’s entitlement to remuneration is generally governed by the company’s articles of association or by specific resolutions or contracts that clearly provide for such remuneration. Expressions such as "indemnify for all costs and expenses" are ordinarily construed, in context, as covering liabilities and out-of-pocket expenses incurred in carrying out authorised tasks, rather than compensation for the director’s own time, unless the wording or surrounding arrangements clearly indicate otherwise. Where the articles prescribe how directors are to be remunerated, claims for additional or alternative remuneration, including on a quantum meruit basis, are typically not available if they would circumvent those prescribed mechanisms.
Party_Details.Application: The director relied on board resolutions and indemnity documents that referred to indemnification for "all costs and expenses" incurred in performing specified tasks for the company and its related entities, and on prior correspondence in which he proposed a fixed monthly remuneration. The company’s articles separately addressed director remuneration by requiring approval of rates in general meeting and providing for fixed sums for extra services determined by the board. The director’s later claim was framed on a time-costs basis and referenced the resolutions and indemnities rather than a specific remuneration resolution under the articles. The wording of the resolutions and indemnities focused on indemnifying him for costs, expenses and liabilities incurred in the course of acting for the company and related entities, while the articles set out distinct procedures for approving director remuneration and extra-service payments.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court described the appeal as one by a director against a High Court decision refusing his claim for remuneration and recorded that the appeal was dismissed with costs. It noted that the respondent company had earlier instituted proceedings against the director and others for breaches of duty as a director, and that the company’s claim was later discontinued, leaving only the director’s counterclaim. The trial judge interpreted the phrase "costs and expenses" in the resolutions as covering out-of-pocket expenses but not director remuneration, and rejected a quantum meruit claim, relying on Re Richmond Gate Property Co Ltd. The judge accepted evidence from Yusuf Jumabhoy that he did not agree to the remuneration proposal in the 14 May 1996 letter and concluded that the board had not agreed, via the resolutions, to pay remuneration for the director’s time. The judge also observed that if the director contended that "costs and expenses" were intended to include remuneration, he should have produced evidence from directors involved in the deliberations, which he did not. On appeal, the court framed the central question as whether the resolutions and indemnities amounted to an agreement to pay time costs. It endorsed an objective approach to construction, citing Chitty on Contracts and authorities such as Investors Compensation Scheme Ltd v West Bromwich Building Society, Mills v Cannon Brewery Co Ltd and Mannai Investment Co Ltd v Eagle Star Life Assurance Co Ltd, emphasising that dictionary meanings alone are insufficient and that context and background are critical. The court agreed with the trial judge that, in context, "costs and expenses" in the resolutions did not extend to director remuneration but were limited to liabilities and expenses incurred in carrying out assigned tasks. It stressed the significance of the word "indemnify" preceding "all costs and expenses", reasoning that "costs" referred to debts payable to third parties, not to the director’s own remuneration, and described characterising remuneration as a "cost" incurred by the director as contrived. The court further held that construing "costs" to include remuneration would be inconsistent with Articles 77 and 80 of the company’s articles of association, which required general meeting approval for director remuneration and board determination of fixed sums for extra services. The director’s claim did not meet these requirements, as it was not for a fixed sum approved in general meeting and no time rate had been indicated to or approved by the board. On quantum meruit, the court accepted that the board had requested the director to undertake tasks but applied Re Richmond Gate and Guinness Plc v Saunders, noting that where articles expressly regulate director remuneration, there is no room for a quantum meruit claim that would bypass those provisions. It discussed Lord Templeman’s and Lord Goff’s reasoning in Guinness, including the possibility of an equitable allowance in limited circumstances, as in Boardman v Phipps, but concluded that even assuming such jurisdiction existed for company directors, the director had not shown why an allowance should be granted. The court considered that a time-costs claim could place the director in a position of conflict with the company’s interests, as slower work would increase his remuneration. It therefore rejected the quantum meruit and equitable allowance arguments. On costs, the court explained that the trial judge had ordered an inquiry by the Registrar to determine whether any claimed expenses were in fact incurred on behalf of the company, recognising that the director was entitled to reimbursement of properly incurred expenses under the resolutions and that the company did not dispute that principle. The court clarified that the trial judge had not decided which expense items were allowable and that the costs awarded to the company at first instance related only to the remuneration claim, not to the expense inquiry. It rejected the argument that the director had partially succeeded on expenses for purposes of costs, and upheld the costs order in favour of the company.

Metadata.Coram: Chao Hick Tin JA; Lai Siu Chiu J; Yong Pung How CJ

Party_Details.Name: Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd

Facts.Text: Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd was incorporated in May 1991 to hold the Jumabhoy family’s stake in Scotts Holding Ltd, a public listed company.
Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd held the Jumabhoy family’s stake in Scotts Holding Ltd, a public listed company.
One of the main subsidiaries of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd was Lion City Holdings Pte Ltd.
Lion City Holdings Pte Ltd was a subsidiary of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd.
By the mid-1990s, Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd experienced a financial crisis that led to protracted legal proceedings involving members of the Jumabhoy family.
By the mid-1990s, banks that had granted facilities to Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd became alarmed about the company’s financial position.
By the mid-1990s, members of the Jumabhoy family were concerned that banks might take action against Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd.

On 27 July 1996, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd resolved to appoint Yusuf Jumabhoy and Rafiq Jumabhoy from among the directors of the company to review the company’s investment in LCH, determine steps to safeguard the company’s investment in LCH and amounts owing to or guaranteed by the company for LCH debts, and exercise all powers of the board in connection therewith.

On 27 July 1996, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd resolved that the company shall indemnify Yusuf Jumabhoy and Rafiq Jumabhoy for all costs and expenses incurred by them personally in respect of their appointment.
On 27 July 1996, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd appointed Rajah & Tann as the company’s solicitors to act for the company in relation to all matters regarding LCH.

On 6 August 1996, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd passed a resolution in all material respects similar to the 27 July 1996 resolution, except that it related to SIS’s investment in SHL.

On 18 June 1997, the board of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd resolved that, in consideration for his taking action for the recovery of the assets of the company, Mr Rafiq Jumabhoy be fully indemnified for all costs and expenses incurred and against all legal liability as far as permitted by law with regards to LCH, its subsidiaries and associates, and in the pursuit of any action to be taken on behalf of SIS/LCH as advised by the company’s solicitors.

On 30 June 1997, an indemnity was issued in favour of Rafiq Jumabhoy stating that, in consideration of his agreeing to reasonably assist at the cost and expense of the relevant company and/or ourselves in the recovery of the assets of SIS, LCH and/or its subsidiaries and associated companies, including without limitation certain named companies, the prosecution of any claim which any company of the LCH Group may have, and any reasonable action in connection with the affairs of any company of the LCH Group as may be advised by the company’s solicitors, the issuers agreed to indemnify him and keep him indemnified at all times, to the extent permitted by law, out of their assets against all costs and expenses and liabilities incurred or suffered by him in connection with any of the foregoing, until revoked in writing.

Article 77 of the Articles of Association of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd provided that directors other than a managing director shall be paid remuneration for their services out of company funds at such rate as the company in general meeting may from time to time determine, with such remuneration divided among them as the directors determine or, in default, equally.

Article 80 of the Articles of Association of Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd provided that each director shall be paid travelling, hotel and other expenses properly incurred, and that if any director is called upon to perform extra services for company purposes, the company shall remunerate that director by a fixed sum determined by the directors, which may be in addition to or in substitution for his share in the general remuneration.

In response to the counterclaim, Scotts Investments (Singapore) Pte Ltd took the position that none of the expense items claimed by Rafiq Jumabhoy were incurred on behalf of the company, while not disputing that properly incurred expenses on behalf of the company would be reimbursable.

Party_Details.Issue: Whether the resolutions and indemnities referring to indemnification for "all costs and expenses" required the company to pay a director for time spent on company affairs in addition to reimbursing out-of-pocket expenses, and whether the director could obtain remuneration on a quantum meruit basis despite the company’s articles of association specifying how directors are to be remunerated.
Party_Details.Rule: When a company’s articles of association prescribe the mechanisms and approvals required for director remuneration, payments to directors for their services generally must comply with those provisions. Wording in board resolutions or indemnities that a company will "indemnify" a director for "all costs and expenses" is ordinarily interpreted, in context, as covering third-party liabilities and out-of-pocket expenses incurred in performing authorised tasks, not as creating a separate entitlement to remuneration for the director’s own time, unless clearly stated. Claims for additional remuneration, including on a quantum meruit or equitable allowance basis, are constrained where they would conflict with or bypass the remuneration framework set out in the articles.
Party_Details.Application: The company had adopted articles that required general meeting approval for director remuneration and board determination of fixed sums for extra services. Later board resolutions and indemnities used indemnity language to cover "all costs and expenses" and liabilities incurred by the director in carrying out specified tasks for the company and related entities. The director’s counterclaim sought substantial time-based remuneration by relying on those resolutions and indemnities, rather than on a remuneration resolution passed in accordance with the articles. The company maintained that the claimed expenses were not incurred on its behalf, while accepting that properly incurred expenses would be reimbursable under the resolutions. The dispute therefore centred on whether the indemnity wording extended beyond reimbursement of out-of-pocket costs to include payment for the director’s time, and whether any additional remuneration could be claimed outside the procedures set out in the articles.
Party_Details.Judicial_Reasoning_Log: The court upheld the High Court’s refusal of the director’s remuneration claim and dismissed the appeal with costs. It accepted the trial judge’s interpretation that "costs and expenses" in the resolutions did not include director remuneration and that the director was not entitled to payment on a quantum meruit basis. The court emphasised an objective approach to construing the resolutions and indemnities, focusing on how a reasonable person with the relevant background would understand the words, and endorsed the trial judge’s caution against relying solely on broad dictionary meanings. It reasoned that the term "indemnify" preceding "all costs and expenses" indicated that the company was undertaking to cover debts and liabilities to third parties incurred by the directors in performing their tasks, not to compensate them for their own time. The court considered it artificial to characterise a director’s remuneration as a "cost" incurred by the director. It further held that interpreting "costs" to include remuneration would conflict with Articles 77 and 80 of the company’s articles of association, which required general meeting approval for director remuneration and board determination of fixed sums for extra services. The director’s time-costs claim did not comply with these requirements, as no general meeting had approved a rate and no fixed sum for extra services had been determined by the board. On quantum meruit, the court applied Re Richmond Gate and Guinness Plc v Saunders, concluding that where articles expressly regulate director remuneration, there is no scope for an implied contract or quantum meruit claim that would circumvent those provisions. While acknowledging Lord Goff’s discussion of equitable allowances in Boardman v Phipps, the court found that even if such a jurisdiction could apply to company directors, it should not be exercised here because the director’s time-costs claim would place his interests in conflict with the company’s interest in efficient performance. The court therefore confirmed that the company was not required to pay the claimed remuneration. Regarding expenses, the court noted that the trial judge had ordered an inquiry by the Registrar to determine whether any of the claimed items were properly incurred on behalf of the company, and that the company did not dispute its obligation to reimburse properly incurred expenses under the resolutions. The court held that the costs order in favour of the company at first instance related to the remuneration issue alone and rejected the argument that the director’s position on expenses amounted to partial success warranting a different costs order.
